summary: 1936-1939 civil war between the Nationalists, a coalition of army rebels led by Francisco Franco and backed by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy, and the Republicans (or Loyalists), the leftist Popular Front government backed by the Soviet Union and international volunteers of the International Brigades. Sparked by a July 1936 military coup against the Second Spanish Republic, it featured the German Condor Legion's bombing of Guernica, the shipment of Spain's gold reserves to Moscow, and a Nationalist victory that installed Franco as dictator for 36 years. Extremely heavily clued through the war's outsized cultural legacy: it is the setting of Ernest Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls and George Orwell's Homage to Catalonia, and it inspired Pablo Picasso's Guernica and Robert Capa's photograph The Falling Soldier.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: A civil war fought from 1936 to 1939 between the Nationalists, fascist rebels led by Francisco Franco and backed by Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy, and the Republicans (or Loyalists), the leftist Popular Front government of the Second Spanish Republic, backed by the Soviet Union and the volunteer International Brigades. It ended with a Nationalist victory that installed Franco as dictator. It is most often identified through the sheer volume of literature and art it inspired, and through the 1936 assassination of the poet Federico Garcia Lorca by Franco's supporters.

clues.clue: Civil war (1936-1939) between the fascist Nationalists, led by Francisco Franco, and the Republicans (Loyalists) of the Second Spanish Republic; ended in a Nationalist victory that brought Franco to power
examples: Battles at the Ebro and Guernica were fought during, for 10 points, what war that brought Francisco Franco to power in an Iberian country?
name this 1930s conflict won by Francisco Franco's fascists.

clues.clue: The poet Federico Garcia Lorca was assassinated by Franco's supporters in 1936 during this war
examples: name this war during which Federico Garcia Lorca was assassinated.
Federico Garcia Lorca was assassinated during what conflict?

clues.clue: Setting of Ernest Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls and George Orwell's Homage to Catalonia
examples: name this war, the setting of Homage to Catalonia and For Whom the Bell Tolls, during which the author Federico Garcia Lorca was killed by Franco's supporters.
Robert Jordan blows up a bridge in, for 10 points, what war, the setting of Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls and Orwell's Homage to Catalonia?

works.name: Background and Causes
works.description: The war grew out of instability within the Second Spanish Republic during the early 1930s, as right-wing and left-wing violence escalated in the years before the July 1936 military uprising.

clues.clue: The CEDA (Confederacion Espanola de Derechas Autonomas)'s fall from power prompted a coup led by General Jose Sanjurjo
examples: One of its causes was the CEDA's fall from power, which prompted a coup led by Jose Sanjurjo.

clues.clue: Presaged by a miners' strike that was supported in parliament by the feminist Margarita Nelken
examples: This war was presaged by a miners' strike that was supported in parliament by the feminist Margarita Nelken.

clues.clue: Just before the war's outbreak, outrage followed the summary execution of anyone who owned a gun in the town of Casas Viejas
examples: Just before the start of this conflict, outrage followed the summary execution of anyone who owned a gun in the town of Casas Viejas.

works.name: The Nationalist Faction and the July 1936 Coup
works.description: The Nationalists were a coalition of army generals and right-wing movements who launched the July 1936 uprising against the Republic. Its early leadership, Jose Sanjurjo and Emilio Mola, both died in plane crashes within a year of the coup, leaving Francisco Franco as sole Nationalist leader; Franco led the victorious Falange and ruled Spain as dictator for 36 years afterward.

clues.clue: General Emilio Mola coined the term 'fifth column' to describe secret Nationalist sympathizers within Republican-held cities, notably during the siege of Madrid
examples: During this war, Emilio Mola coined the term "fifth column."
This man coined the term "fifth column" during the siege of Madrid.

clues.clue: Mola died in a plane crash in 1937, following the earlier death of fellow general Jose Sanjurjo (also in a plane crash, reportedly weighed down by luggage he insisted on bringing back to Spain), leaving Franco as sole Nationalist leader
examples: Both Sanjurjo and this fellow general died in plane crashes, leaving Franco as the sole Nationalist leader.
this Nationalist leader died in a plane crash, reportedly because he insisted on taking heavy luggage back to Spain with him.

clues.clue: Sanjurjo, nicknamed the 'Lion of the Rif' for earlier defeating Abd el-Krim's guerrilla resistance in Morocco, served as a general for Franco's Nationalist faction
examples: Sanjurjo was a general for Francisco Franco's Nationalist faction in this conflict.

clues.clue: Franco led the victorious Falange and became Generalissimo, ruling Spain as dictator for 36 years until his 1975 death, when he was succeeded by Juan Carlos I; he had merged the Falange with the Carlist movement into a single party
examples: This Generalissimo was dictator of Spain until his 1975 death.
This victor of the Spanish Civil War seized power and combined the Falange with Carlism. He ruled as dictator of Spain for the next 36 years.

works.name: The Republican Faction and Factional Strife
works.description: The Republican side was a fractious coalition of the Popular Front government, Trotskyist and anarchist militias, and international volunteers, whose internal divisions occasionally erupted into open violence, as in Barcelona's 1937 May Events.

clues.clue: The Trotskyist POUM militia and the anarchist CNT clashed with the government's Assault Guard in Barcelona's May Events
examples: During this war, the Assault Guard clashed with the POUM and the CNT in the May Events.

clues.clue: Buenaventura Durruti's anarchist Durruti Column, which included the philosopher Simone Weil among roughly 3,000 volunteers operating without direct command, fought to retake Zaragoza from General Mola; Durruti was killed in 1936
examples: Name this man who fought alongside philosopher Simone Weil in a namesake military unit of 3,000 anarchists that operated without any direct command.
The Durruti Column fought in this conflict, trying to retake Zaragoza from General Emilio Mola.

clues.clue: President Manuel Azana's Republican government was eventually driven from Madrid and then Barcelona
examples: The Spanish Civil War broke out during the term of this leftist President, who ordered Spain's gold reserve to be sent to the Soviet Union before his government was driven out of Madrid and then Barcelona.

works.name: Foreign Intervention and the International Brigades
works.description: Both sides depended heavily on foreign support: the Nationalists on German and Italian arms and airpower, the Republicans on Soviet aid and volunteer International Brigades, even as other powers like Britain and France officially maintained non-intervention.

clues.clue: The Nationalists received supplies from Nazi Germany through the dummy companies HISMA and ROWAK, and were assisted militarily by the German Condor Legion
examples: One side of this war received supplies from an allied country through the dummy companies HISMA and ROWAK.

clues.clue: International Brigades of foreign volunteers fought for the Republicans, including units named for James Connolly and Abraham Lincoln, the Canadian Mackenzie-Papineau Battalion, and the Italian Garibaldi Brigade
examples: International supporters of one side during this conflict fought in units named for James Connolly and Abraham Lincoln as part of the International Brigades.
Name this conflict, during which international units, including the Abraham Lincoln Brigade and Garibaldi Brigade, fought on the Republican side.

clues.clue: Irish volunteers fought on both sides: the Republican-aligned Connolly Column was later the subject of a Christy Moore song inspired by a 1979 book by Michael O'Riordan, while Eoin O'Duffy's fascist Blueshirts merged with the Greenshirts to form the Irish Brigade in support of Franco
examples: A Christy Moore song about Irish volunteers who fought in this war was inspired by a 1979 book by Michael O'Riordan.
O'Duffy's Blueshirts merged with the National Corporate Party's Greenshirts to form the Irish Brigade during this war in support of Francisco Franco.

clues.clue: Piracy against merchant shipping during the war was addressed at the Nyon Conference, and the war caused the cancellation of the alternative 'People's Olympiad' in Barcelona
examples: Piracy during this war was addressed at the Nyon Conference, and this war caused the cancellation of the People's Olympiad.

clues.clue: France's leftist Popular Front government, headed by Leon Blum, fractured over whether to intervene in the war
examples: The Popular Front fell apart over whether or not to intervene in this conflict, which saw Hitler send the Condor Legion.

works.name: Key Battles and Sieges
works.description: The war's major military turning points included a years-long siege of the Republican capital, a costly Republican offensive that briefly retook and then lost a provincial capital, and a final Republican defeat along the Ebro river.

clues.clue: The siege of Madrid lasted for most of the war; the defenders' rallying cry '¡No pasaran!' ('They shall not pass!') was popularized by a speech from Dolores Ibarruri, nicknamed 'La Pasionaria' ('Passionflower')
examples: A key siege during this war saw a woman called "Passionflower" give the non-French "They shall not pass!" speech.
The Siege of Madrid lasted for most of this conflict, in which Republican forces were eventually defeated by the Nationalists under Francisco Franco.

clues.clue: At the Battle of Teruel, Republican forces briefly captured the Nationalist provincial capital before Antonio Aranda led a successful counterattack; following the loss, Prime Minister Juan Negrin ordered an unsuccessful attack on a river bend, and the Republican side ultimately surrendered after failing to recapture the city
examples: Following this war's Battle of Teruel, an unsuccessful attack on the bend of a river was ordered by Prime Minister Juan Negrin.
The losing side of this war surrendered after Juan Negrim's government failed to recapture Tereul.

clues.clue: Republican forces were ultimately crushed at the Battle of the Ebro
examples: This war, in which Republican forces were crushed at the Battle of the Ebro, also included the Condor Legion's bombing of the Basque city of Guernica.

clues.clue: The Gipuzkoa Campaign helped cut off and economically strangle the Republicans, contributing to the Nationalist victory in the War in the North
examples: Name this conflict, which may have become economically unwinnable for the losers after they were cut off by the Gipuzkoa Campaign, which helped the victors win the War in the North.

works.name: The Bombing of Guernica
works.description: One of the war's most infamous incidents: the German Condor Legion's 1937 aerial bombardment of the Basque town of Guernica, which shocked international opinion and later inspired Pablo Picasso's painting of the same name.

clues.clue: The German Condor Legion, codenamed Operation Rugen, bombed the sacred Basque town of Guernica in 1937, destroying its sacred 600-year-old oak tree
examples: During this war, the German Condor Legion bombed the Basque town of Guernica.
An aerial bombardment in this war destroyed a town's sacred 600-year-old oak tree.

clues.clue: George Steer's reporting on the bombing for The Times helped foster international outrage
examples: George Steer's reporting for The Times helped foster international outrage for an incident during this conflict.

works.name: The Moscow Gold Affair
works.description: Soon after the war began, the Republican government shipped most of Spain's gold reserves to the Soviet Union in exchange for arms, an episode later dubbed the Moscow Gold affair and used by Franco's supporters to paint the Republic as a Soviet puppet.

clues.clue: The Republican government shipped roughly 510 tons (nearly three-fourths of the country's reserves) of gold to Moscow soon after the war's outbreak in exchange for Soviet arms; NKVD agent Alexander Orlov oversaw the transport
examples: One side in this war sent 510 tons of gold to Moscow soon after its outbreak.
After the outbreak of this war, Alexander Orlov transported a gold reserve to Moscow.

clues.clue: President Manuel Azana ordered the gold transfer before his government was driven from Madrid and then Barcelona
examples: The Spanish Civil War broke out during the term of this leftist President, who ordered Spain's gold reserve to be sent to the Soviet Union before his government was driven out of Madrid and then Barcelona.

clues.clue: As Minister of Finance, Juan Negrin persuaded Prime Minister Francisco Largo Caballero to carry out the gold transfer, for which Franco's supporters later villainized him; Negrin subsequently became prime minister himself
examples: Francisco Franco's supporters villainized past prime minister Juan Negrin for performing this action during the war during his time as Minister of Finance.
This Republican minister of finance persuaded Francisco Largo Caballero to make the gold transfer to Moscow.

clues.clue: The transfer culminated when four ships, whose loading was supervised by Francisco Mendez Aspe, landed the gold in Odessa
examples: This event culminated when four ships, whose loading was supervised by Francisco Mendez Aspe, landed in Odessa.

works.name: Aftermath: Franco's Dictatorship
works.description: The Nationalist victory installed Franco as dictator, and the war's memory and repression shaped Spanish culture for decades afterward.

clues.clue: The Valley of the Fallen, near Madrid, was built to honor the war's dead
examples: The Valley of the Fallen near this city honors the dead from the war during which that siege took place.

clues.clue: The development of Basque-language literature almost entirely ceased for two decades in the war's aftermath
examples: The development of Basque literature almost ceased for two decades after this war.

works.name: Literature of the War
works.description: Few 20th-century conflicts produced a denser literary canon: the war is the direct setting of major novels by Ernest Hemingway, George Orwell, and Jean-Paul Sartre, of an entire generation of Spanish-language fiction, and of some of the era's most famous political poetry.

clues.clue: Ernest Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls follows the American dynamiter Robert Jordan, who joins a Republican guerrilla band led by Anselmo, Pilar, and her husband Pablo to blow up a bridge; Pilar reads Jordan's palms, and he falls in love with Maria, a former rape victim
examples: During this war, Anselmo, Pilar, and Pablo lead a guerrilla band plotting to blow up a bridge and are joined by Robert Jordan.
gets his palms read by Pilar, Pablo's wife.

clues.clue: George Orwell's memoir Homage to Catalonia describes how, while fighting with the POUM militia, he was shot through the neck by a sniper and failed to free his friend Georges Kopp from prison before escaping Spain
examples: A memoir of this conflict describes how its author survived a sniper wound to the neck and fought alongside Georges Kopp.
the author recounts being shot in the neck by a sniper and failing to free Georges Kopp from prison.

clues.clue: Orwell's publisher Victor Gollancz, who had earlier commissioned Orwell's The Road to Wigan Pier, refused to publish Homage to Catalonia; Orwell derided him as 'part of the Communism-racket'
examples: Orwell derided Gollancz as "part of the Communism-racket" when he refused to publish Homage to Catalonia.

clues.clue: Jean-Paul Sartre's short story 'The Wall' is set in a frigid hospital cellar where a Belgian priest tends to prisoners awaiting execution; its protagonist Pablo Ibbieta unwittingly reveals the hiding place of his ally Ramon Gris, who is found hiding in a cemetery and shot by Nationalists
examples: a Belgian priest tends to three prisoners fated to be shot against the title structure.
In Sartre's 1939 story The Wall, Ramon Gris is accidentally betrayed by Pablo Ibbieta during this war, and ends up shot by Nationalists.

clues.clue: Camilo Jose Cela's The Family of Pascual Duarte, written as two 'found' prison letters, ends with its protagonist's death during the war after he murders El Estirao, the lover of his sister Rosario and his wife Lola; Cela's later novel The Hive is set at the war's end
examples: Pascal Duarte's life ends during this conflict in a novel by Camilo Jose Cela.
Camilo Jose Cela, the author of The Family of Pascual Duarte, set The Hive at the end of this war.

clues.clue: Carmen Laforet's Nada, set in the war's aftermath, follows the orphan Andrea as she moves into her grandmother's dilapidated home and befriends Ena at university
examples: An existential novel set in this conflict's aftermath centers on the orphan Andrea moving to her grandmother's run-down home and befriending Ena at university.

clues.clue: Merce Rodoreda's The Time of the Doves follows Natalia through troubled relationships with Quimet and Antoni during the war
examples: Natalia struggles in relationships with Quimet and Antoni in The Time of the Doves, a novel set in this conflict.

clues.clue: The 'Generation of '36' of Spanish writers was primarily active during the war
examples: The Generation of '36 primarily wrote during this conflict.

clues.clue: In Muriel Spark's The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie, the title teacher encourages her student Joyce Emily to fight and die in the war
examples: Miss Jean Brodie inspires Joyce Emily to go fight and die in this conflict.
Miss Brodie encourages her student Joyce Emily to fight in this conflict, leading to Joyce Emily's death.

clues.clue: Maria Duenas's novel The Time in Between features a seamstress who stitches a secret code into her dresses to aid one side in the war
examples: A seamstress stitches a secret code into her dresses to aid one side in this conflict in Maria Duenas's novel The Time In Between.

clues.clue: Carlos Ruiz Zafon's The Shadow of the Wind is set in the war's aftermath
examples: The novel The Shadow of the Wind is set in the aftermath of this war, in which George Orwell served.

clues.clue: Arthur Koestler's memoir Spanish Testament describes his imprisonment during the war, which he later drew on to chronicle Rubashov's confinement (with Joseph Stalin implicitly represented as 'Number One') in his novel Darkness at Noon
examples: This author described his imprisonment during the war in his book Spanish Testament, and drew on his time in prison to chronicle Rubashov's confinement in his novel Darkness at Noon.

clues.clue: Danilo Kis's story collection A Tomb for Boris Davidovich includes a story describing Verschoyle's actions during the war
examples: A story from A Tomb for Boris Davidovich describes Verschoyle's actions in this conflict.

clues.clue: Wartime Spanish theater included Enrique Jardiel Poncela's comedy Four Hearts with Brake and Reverse, Bertolt Brecht's play Senora Carrar's Rifles (starring Helene Weigel), and George Leeson's This Trampled Earth, written after Leeson's own service in the war; simple didactic plays of the period were known as 'urgent theater,' and playwrights Max Aub and Rafael Alberti were exiled after the war
examples: The comedic play Four Hearts with Brake and Reverse was premiered during this conflict.
It's not the Thirty Years' War, but Helene Weigel played the title character in a Bertolt Brecht play titled for her "rifles" set during this conflict.

clues.clue: Historian Paul Preston controversially labeled the war a 'holocaust'
examples: Paul Preston controversially labelled this war a "holocaust" in a history of it.

clues.clue: Pablo Neruda's poem 'I'm Explaining a Few Things' describes the war's toll on Spain, ending with the refrain 'Come and see the blood in the streets!'
examples: A poem set during this conflict ends with the line "Come and see the blood in the streets!" and is titled "I'm Explaining a Few Things."
Pablo Neruda's poem "I Explain a Few Things" is about this conflict.

clues.clue: Cesar Vallejo's poem 'Spain, Take This Cup From Me' addresses 'children, sons of fighters' and warns the war will bring an 'age of concave temples'
examples: A poem which warns this conflict will bring about an "age of concave temples" is partly titled "Take This Cup From Me."
Vallejo wrote about "Children, / sons of fighters" in a poem about this conflict asking its central country to "Take This Cup From Me."

clues.clue: W.H. Auden's poem 'Spain' repeats the refrain 'to-day the struggle' and ends 'The stars are dead. The animals will not look'
examples: Auden poem that repeats, "to-day the struggle."

clues.clue: Sylvia Townsend Warner, serving with the Red Cross, contributed poems (including 'Journey to Barcelona') to a war anthology edited by Stephen Spender
examples: A collection about this country features poems written by Sylvia Townsend Warner while serving with the Red Cross; Stephen Spender edited that collection.

clues.clue: Stephen Spender's autobiography World Within World describes him chasing after an ex-lover who went to fight in the war
examples: In his autobiography World Within World, Stephen Spender describes how he chased an ex-lover who went to fght in this confict.

works.name: Art of the War
works.description: The war, and especially the bombing of Guernica, inspired some of the 20th century's most famous visual art, above all Pablo Picasso's mural-sized painting Guernica.

clues.clue: Pablo Picasso's Guernica, a black-and-white mural-sized painting responding to the bombing, depicts a stabbed horse screaming in pain, a mother holding her dead child beneath a bull, and a flower growing from a disembodied arm gripping a broken dagger
examples: That black-and-white painting also includes a stabbed horse screaming in pain, and a mother holding her dead child under a bull.
name this war that inspired Pablo Picasso's Guernica.

clues.clue: Picasso also produced an etching series he claimed predicted the war, in which a shriveled head emerges from a knife-shaped leg grasped by an arm that itself emerges from another limb
examples: In a painting whose artist claimed was a prediction of this conflict, a shriveled head emerges from a knife-shaped leg being grasped by an arm that itself emerges from another limb.

clues.clue: Joan Miro was commissioned to paint the now-lost mural The Reaper, depicting a sickle-wielding Catalan peasant, shortly after the war's outbreak
examples: That quote's author, Joan Miro, was commissioned to paint the mural The Reaper shortly after the outbreak of this conflict.

clues.clue: Salvador Dali's Soft Construction with Boiled Beans (Premonition of Civil War) was inspired by the conflict
examples: This war inspired Soft Construction with Boiled Beans and an abstract expressionist "elegy" by Robert Motherwell.
which inspired Salvador Dali's Soft Construction with Boiled Beans and Pablo Picasso's Guernica.

clues.clue: Robert Motherwell painted a series of abstract expressionist 'Elegies' using varied arrangements of black and white ovals and squares
examples: A series memorializing this war consists of different arrangements of black and white ovals and squares.

clues.clue: Alexander Calder's Mercury Fountain was likewise inspired by the war
examples: It also inspired Calder's Mercury Fountain and Picasso's Guernica.

clues.clue: The artist Leonora Carrington, who used eggs to depict eggs in her tempera paintings The Giantess and The Kitchen Garden on the Eyot, wrote that the egg was 'the dividing line between the Big and the Small' during a stay at a sanatorium during the war
examples: Carrington wrote that the egg was "the dividing line between the Big and the Small" during her stay at a sanatorium during this conflict.

works.name: Photography of the War
works.description: The war is considered one of the first conflicts extensively documented by photojournalists, above all Robert Capa and Gerda Taro, whose images became defining icons of the conflict.

clues.clue: Robert Capa's photograph The Falling Soldier depicts a Loyalist soldier recoiling as he appears to be shot in the head, his rifle slipping from his hand; Capa may have staged the photo
examples: The best-known image of a war in this country depicts a rifle slipping out of a man's hand as he seems to recoil from being shot in the head.
Robert Capa may have staged his photograph The Falling Soldier during what country's 1930s civil war?

clues.clue: German photojournalist Gerda Taro, who died during a battle in Spain, made her name covering the war's frontlines; her photographs include Woman training for a Republican militia, depicting a woman in heels kneeling on a beach and aiming a pistol
examples: A photograph taken in this country depicts a woman wearing heels who kneels on a beach while aiming a pistol.
The female German photojournalist Gerda Taro died during a battle in this country.

clues.clue: Nearly 4,000 smuggled photographs from the war, including work by Capa, Taro, and Polish photojournalist David 'Chim' Seymour (using the pseudonym of a fictitious American journalist), were discovered in 2010 in three cardboard boxes in a Mexico City closet
examples: In 2010, nearly 4000 smuggled photographs taken in this country were discovered in three cardboard boxes in a closet in Mexico City.

clues.clue: The war's toll was also depicted in Henri Cartier-Bresson's photograph Children Playing in the Ruins
examples: The Falling Soldier depicts a death during this conflict, which was also depicted in Children Playing in the Ruins.

comprehensive_summary: The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) grew out of instability within the Second Spanish Republic, including the CEDA's fall from power (which prompted an earlier coup attempt by General Jose Sanjurjo), a miners' strike backed by the feminist Margarita Nelken, and the summary execution of gun owners in Casas Viejas. In July 1936 a coalition of army generals launched a coup against the Republic, pitting the Nationalists, led first by Sanjurjo and Emilio Mola (both of whom died in plane crashes within the war's first year) and then by Francisco Franco alone, against the Republicans (or Loyalists), the Popular Front government under President Manuel Azana and Prime Ministers Francisco Largo Caballero and Juan Negrin. Mola coined the term 'fifth column' for secret Nationalist sympathizers within besieged Republican cities such as Madrid, whose defenders rallied around Dolores Ibarruri's 'They shall not pass!' speech during a years-long siege. The Republican side was itself divided: the Trotskyist POUM militia (in which George Orwell fought) and the anarchist CNT clashed with the government's Assault Guard in Barcelona's 1937 May Events, while Buenaventura Durruti's anarchist Durruti Column, which included the philosopher Simone Weil, fought to retake Zaragoza. Both sides depended on foreign backing: the Nationalists on the German Condor Legion and Italian aid funneled through dummy companies like HISMA and ROWAK, the Republicans on International Brigades such as the Abraham Lincoln Brigade, the Mackenzie-Papineau Battalion, and the Garibaldi Brigade, and on Soviet arms purchased with roughly 510 tons of Spain's gold reserves shipped to Moscow (the Moscow Gold affair) via NKVD agent Alexander Orlov and finance minister Juan Negrin. Irish volunteers fought on both sides, from the Republican-aligned Connolly Column to Eoin O'Duffy's pro-Franco Irish Brigade. Key military turning points included the Republicans' costly capture and loss of Teruel, their final defeat at the Battle of the Ebro, and the Nationalists' Gipuzkoa Campaign in the War in the North; the war's most infamous single incident was the German Condor Legion's 1937 bombing of the Basque town of Guernica (codenamed Operation Rugen), reported internationally by George Steer. The Nationalist victory installed Franco as a 36-year dictator and built the Valley of the Fallen to honor the war dead, while Basque literature went dormant for two decades. Few conflicts have inspired as dense a cultural record: the war is the direct setting of Ernest Hemingway's For Whom the Bell Tolls, George Orwell's Homage to Catalonia, and Jean-Paul Sartre's short story 'The Wall,' as well as Spanish-language novels by Camilo Jose Cela, Carmen Laforet, and Merce Rodoreda, and later ones by Muriel Spark, Maria Duenas, and Carlos Ruiz Zafon; it produced political poetry by Pablo Neruda, Cesar Vallejo, W.H. Auden, and Sylvia Townsend Warner. Visually, the bombing of Guernica inspired Pablo Picasso's mural-sized painting Guernica, alongside Salvador Dali's Soft Construction with Boiled Beans, Joan Miro's mural The Reaper, Robert Motherwell's Elegy series, and Alexander Calder's Mercury Fountain. The war was also a landmark for photojournalism, producing Robert Capa's The Falling Soldier and the work of Gerda Taro, later recovered in the 'Mexican Suitcase' of smuggled negatives.
recursive_suggestions: Peninsular War -- a distinct, much earlier (1807-1814) Napoleonic-era conflict in Spain and Portugal; one fetched tossup (2022 ARCADIA) answers to this instead and explicitly rejects 'Spanish Civil War' -- excluded from this analysis
Francisco Franco -- Nationalist victor and 36-year dictator of Spain; warrants his own History biography page
George Orwell -- Homage to Catalonia recounts his Spanish Civil War service; his broader literary career (Animal Farm, 1984, The Road to Wigan Pier) warrants its own Literature page
Ernest Hemingway -- For Whom the Bell Tolls is set in this war; his broader career warrants its own Literature page
Pablo Picasso -- Guernica is among his most famous paintings; warrants his own Visual Fine Arts page
Robert Capa -- photographer of The Falling Soldier and co-founder of Magnum Photos; warrants his own Fine Arts (Photography) page
Federico Garcia Lorca -- poet and playwright assassinated during the war; warrants his own Literature page
